main_paper.raw: {"methods":["LLaMoCo: an instruction-tuning framework for LLMs to generate optimization code in a code-to-code manner","Comprehensive instruction set with code-style problem descriptions and expert-level optimization code outputs","Two-phase learning strategy: contrastive learning-based warm-up followed by sequence-to-sequence instruction tuning"],"problems":["Low computational efficiency and high resource consumption in existing LLM-based optimization code generation methods","High sensitivity to prompt design in LLMs for optimization tasks","Lack of domain-specific knowledge in general-purpose LLMs for generating expert-level optimization code","Difficulty in generating robust, instance-specific optimizers for diverse optimization problems"],"datasets":["Custom code-to-code instruction set with problem descriptions (in Python/LaTeX) and expert-level optimizer code (details not fully specified in the excerpt)","Synthetic and realistic optimization problem sets for benchmarking"],"metrics":["Optimization performance (e.g., function value achieved)","Zero-shot generalization performance","Efficiency (e.g., number of rounds/conversations needed)","Robustness"],"results":[{"metric":"Optimization performance (CodeGen-350M tuned by LLaMoCo vs GPT-4 Turbo and others)","value":"LLaMoCo-tuned CodeGen-350M achieves superior performance compared to GPT-4 Turbo and other competitors on both synthetic and realistic problem sets"}],"novelty_claims":["LLaMoCo is the first instruction-tuning framework specifically designed to adapt LLMs for optimization code generation in a code-to-code manner","Introduction of a comprehensive, domain-specific instruction set pairing code-style problem descriptions with expert-level optimizer code","Development of a novel two-phase learning strategy with contrastive learning-based warm-up to enhance convergence and performance","Demonstration that a small, instruction-tuned LLM (CodeGen-350M) can outperform much larger models (e.g., GPT-4 Turbo) on domain-specific optimization code generation tasks","Superior zero-shot generalization, efficiency, and robustness compared to existing methods"]}

methods: LLaMoCo: an instruction-tuning framework for LLMs to generate optimization code in a code-to-code manner
Comprehensive instruction set with code-style problem descriptions and expert-level optimization code outputs
Two-phase learning strategy: contrastive learning-based warm-up followed by sequence-to-sequence instruction tuning
problems: Low computational efficiency and high resource consumption in existing LLM-based optimization code generation methods
High sensitivity to prompt design in LLMs for optimization tasks
Lack of domain-specific knowledge in general-purpose LLMs for generating expert-level optimization code
Difficulty in generating robust, instance-specific optimizers for diverse optimization problems
datasets: Custom code-to-code instruction set with problem descriptions (in Python/LaTeX) and expert-level optimizer code (details not fully specified in the excerpt)
Synthetic and realistic optimization problem sets for benchmarking

results.metric: Optimization performance (CodeGen-350M tuned by LLaMoCo vs GPT-4 Turbo and others)
results.value: LLaMoCo-tuned CodeGen-350M achieves superior performance compared to GPT-4 Turbo and other competitors on both synthetic and realistic problem sets

novelty_claims: LLaMoCo is the first instruction-tuning framework specifically designed to adapt LLMs for optimization code generation in a code-to-code manner
Introduction of a comprehensive, domain-specific instruction set pairing code-style problem descriptions with expert-level optimizer code
Development of a novel two-phase learning strategy with contrastive learning-based warm-up to enhance convergence and performance
Demonstration that a small, instruction-tuned LLM (CodeGen-350M) can outperform much larger models (e.g., GPT-4 Turbo) on domain-specific optimization code generation tasks
Superior zero-shot generalization, efficiency, and robustness compared to existing methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Brute-force sampling of multiple solutions per problem using Mixtral model","Targeted solution generation with subject-specific few-shot prompts","Incorporating reference text solutions into few-shot prompts for code-interpreter style solution synthesis","Construction of OpenMathInstruct-1 dataset by synthesizing code-interpreter solutions for GSM8K and MATH using Mixtral"],"problems":["Lack of large, permissively licensed math instruction tuning datasets for LLMs","Dependence on closed-source, commercially restrictive LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) for generating high-quality synthetic math data","Gap in mathematical reasoning abilities between open-source and closed-source LLMs, limiting open-source data generation","High cost and legal/reproducibility issues with closed-source model-based data generation pipelines"],"datasets":["GSM8K","MATH","OpenMathInstruct-1"],"metrics":["Training Set Coverage (TSC)","GSM8K accuracy","MATH accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Training Set Coverage (TSC) on GSM8K","value":"99.9%"},{"metric":"Training Set Coverage (TSC) on MATH","value":"93%"},{"metric":"GSM8K accuracy (OpenMath-CodeLlama-70B)","value":"84.6%"},{"metric":"MATH accuracy (OpenMath-CodeLlama-70B)","value":"50.7%"}],"novelty_claims":["OpenMathInstruct-1 is the largest (1.8M pairs) permissively licensed math instruction tuning dataset, at least 4x larger than previous datasets.","First to use the open-source Mixtral model for large-scale code-interpreter style solution synthesis for math instruction tuning.","Demonstrates that incorporating reference text solutions into few-shot prompts with open-source LLMs can substantially improve training set coverage and downstream model performance.","Releases all code, models, and the dataset under a commercially permissive license, enabling unrestricted use and reproducibility."]}

methods: Brute-force sampling of multiple solutions per problem using Mixtral model
Targeted solution generation with subject-specific few-shot prompts
Incorporating reference text solutions into few-shot prompts for code-interpreter style solution synthesis
Construction of OpenMathInstruct-1 dataset by synthesizing code-interpreter solutions for GSM8K and MATH using Mixtral
problems: Lack of large, permissively licensed math instruction tuning datasets for LLMs
Dependence on closed-source, commercially restrictive LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) for generating high-quality synthetic math data
Gap in mathematical reasoning abilities between open-source and closed-source LLMs, limiting open-source data generation
High cost and legal/reproducibility issues with closed-source model-based data generation pipelines
novelty_claims: OpenMathInstruct-1 is the largest (1.8M pairs) permissively licensed math instruction tuning dataset, at least 4x larger than previous datasets.
First to use the open-source Mixtral model for large-scale code-interpreter style solution synthesis for math instruction tuning.
Demonstrates that incorporating reference text solutions into few-shot prompts with open-source LLMs can substantially improve training set coverage and downstream model performance.
Releases all code, models, and the dataset under a commercially permissive license, enabling unrestricted use and reproducibility.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Optimization by PROmpting (OPRO): leveraging large language models (LLMs) as optimizers by iteratively generating new solutions (e.g., prompts) using a meta-prompt that includes previous solutions and their scores, and a natural language description of the optimization task."],"problems":["Derivative-free optimization in real-world applications where gradients are unavailable.","Automated prompt optimization for LLMs to maximize task accuracy on natural language tasks.","General optimization tasks such as linear regression and the traveling salesman problem using LLMs as optimizers."],"datasets":["GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021)","Big-Bench Hard (Suzgun et al., 2022)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (on training and test sets)"],"results":[{"metric":"Test accuracy improvement on GSM8K (over human-designed prompts)","value":"up to 8%"},{"metric":"Test accuracy improvement on Big-Bench Hard (over human-designed prompts)","value":"up to 50%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes OPRO, a general framework for using LLMs as optimizers via prompting, applicable to both classic optimization problems and prompt optimization.","Demonstrates that LLMs can iteratively generate improved solutions (e.g., prompts) by leveraging the optimization trajectory, not just editing a single prompt.","Shows that OPRO-optimized prompts can outperform human-designed prompts and transfer to other benchmarks within the same domain.","Highlights that OPRO enables optimization in a derivative-free, black-box setting using only natural language descriptions and solution-score pairs."]}

methods: Optimization by PROmpting (OPRO): leveraging large language models (LLMs) as optimizers by iteratively generating new solutions (e.g., prompts) using a meta-prompt that includes previous solutions and their scores, and a natural language description of the optimization task.
problems: Derivative-free optimization in real-world applications where gradients are unavailable.
Automated prompt optimization for LLMs to maximize task accuracy on natural language tasks.
General optimization tasks such as linear regression and the traveling salesman problem using LLMs as optimizers.
metrics: Task accuracy (on training and test sets)

results.metric: Test accuracy improvement on GSM8K (over human-designed prompts)
results.value: up to 8%

results.metric: Test accuracy improvement on Big-Bench Hard (over human-designed prompts)
results.value: up to 50%

novelty_claims: Proposes OPRO, a general framework for using LLMs as optimizers via prompting, applicable to both classic optimization problems and prompt optimization.
Demonstrates that LLMs can iteratively generate improved solutions (e.g., prompts) by leveraging the optimization trajectory, not just editing a single prompt.
Shows that OPRO-optimized prompts can outperform human-designed prompts and transfer to other benchmarks within the same domain.
Highlights that OPRO enables optimization in a derivative-free, black-box setting using only natural language descriptions and solution-score pairs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["OptiMUS: an LLM-based agent that formulates and solves MILP problems from natural language descriptions","Automated data augmentation via problem rephrasing","Self-fixing of solutions via automated testing and debugging"],"problems":["Automating the formulation and solution of optimization problems (LP and MILP) from natural language descriptions","Bridging the expertise gap in optimization modeling to make optimization more accessible","Improving the solve rate and reliability of LLM-based optimization agents"],"datasets":["NLP4LP: a novel dataset of 52 LP and MILP optimization problems with human-expert formulations, solutions, and code"],"metrics":["Solve rate (number of problems solved)"],"results":[{"metric":"Solve rate improvement over basic LLM prompting","value":"OptiMUS solves nearly twice as many problems as a basic LLM prompting strategy"},{"metric":"Solve rate improvement with data augmentation and self-fixing","value":"91% increase compared to direct prompting"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of OptiMUS, an LLM-based agent that can formulate and solve MILP problems from natural language, including code generation, debugging, and solution validation","Development of NLP4LP, a novel benchmark dataset for LP and MILP problems with natural language descriptions, solutions, and code","Techniques for automated data augmentation (problem rephrasing) and self-fixing (automated testing and debugging) to improve LLM-based optimization modeling","Demonstration that OptiMUS significantly outperforms basic LLM prompting strategies in solve rate"]}

methods: OptiMUS: an LLM-based agent that formulates and solves MILP problems from natural language descriptions
Automated data augmentation via problem rephrasing
Self-fixing of solutions via automated testing and debugging
problems: Automating the formulation and solution of optimization problems (LP and MILP) from natural language descriptions
Bridging the expertise gap in optimization modeling to make optimization more accessible
Improving the solve rate and reliability of LLM-based optimization agents
datasets: NLP4LP: a novel dataset of 52 LP and MILP optimization problems with human-expert formulations, solutions, and code

results.metric: Solve rate improvement over basic LLM prompting
results.value: OptiMUS solves nearly twice as many problems as a basic LLM prompting strategy

results.metric: Solve rate improvement with data augmentation and self-fixing
results.value: 91% increase compared to direct prompting

novelty_claims: Introduction of OptiMUS, an LLM-based agent that can formulate and solve MILP problems from natural language, including code generation, debugging, and solution validation
Development of NLP4LP, a novel benchmark dataset for LP and MILP problems with natural language descriptions, solutions, and code
Techniques for automated data augmentation (problem rephrasing) and self-fixing (automated testing and debugging) to improve LLM-based optimization modeling
Demonstration that OptiMUS significantly outperforms basic LLM prompting strategies in solve rate

budgets","Show that LLMs can be effective in the initial search phase and in limited-budget scenarios, making HPO more accessible"]}

methods: Prompting large language models (LLMs) with dataset and model descriptions to suggest hyperparameter configurations
Iterative refinement of hyperparameter suggestions by LLMs based on model performance feedback
Prompting LLMs to generate training code (e.g., in PyTorch) as a form of hyperparameter optimization
Comparison of LLM-based HPO with traditional methods such as random search and Bayesian optimization
Use of chain-of-thought reasoning in LLM prompts for improved HPO
problems: Automating hyperparameter optimization (HPO) for machine learning models, especially under limited search budgets
Reducing reliance on manual specification of hyperparameter search spaces and bounds
Improving the efficiency and effectiveness of HPO for practitioners with limited expertise or resources
Overcoming the initial search phase challenges in high-dimensional HPO

results.metric: Validation loss (CIFAR-10, Vision Transformers and ResNets, small search budget)
results.value: LLMs were significantly faster and matched or outperformed random search and Bayesian optimization
LLMs effectively minimized loss, exploiting performant regions and exploring untested areas
Code generation by LLMs performed better than random search and was comparable to LLM search with human-specified configurations

novelty_claims: First to systematically explore the use of large language models for hyperparameter optimization by prompting with dataset and model descriptions
Propose treating the code specifying the model as a hyperparameter, allowing LLMs to generate code for HPO, which offers greater flexibility than existing approaches
Demonstrate that LLMs can match or outperform traditional HPO methods like Bayesian optimization within constrained search budgets
Show that LLMs can be effective in the initial search phase and in limited-budget scenarios, making HPO more accessible

methods: EvoLLM: LLM-based Evolution Strategy using a novel prompting strategy (least-to-most sorting of discretized population members, fitness improvement queries)
Integer-based search space discretization for solution representation
Decision transformer-style fitness improvement queries
Instruction fine-tuning of LLMs on teacher algorithm optimization trajectories
problems: Zero-shot black-box optimization using large language models (LLMs)
Inducing LLMs to act as evolutionary optimization algorithms without explicit training on optimization tasks

results.value: EvoLLM robustly outperforms random search and Gaussian Hill Climbing
Smaller LLMs tend to outperform larger ones in this setting
Discretized solution space representation greatly outperforms natural language-based instructions

novelty_claims: First demonstration that LLMs can be used as zero-shot black-box optimization algorithms via in-context learning and prompting, without explicit optimization training.
Introduction of a novel prompting strategy (least-to-most sorting, discretized representation, fitness improvement query) to induce LLMs to act as recombination operators in evolutionary strategies.
Empirical evidence that LLMs can outperform traditional black-box optimization baselines on synthetic and neuroevolution tasks.
Demonstration that instruction fine-tuning on teacher optimization trajectories can further improve LLM-based optimization performance.

methods: Evaluation of large Transformer-based language models (244M to 137B parameters) for program synthesis in Python
Few-shot learning with prompt-based code synthesis
Fine-tuning language models on small, task-specific code datasets
Interactive dialog with human feedback to iteratively improve code synthesis
Error analysis and semantic grounding by training models to predict program execution results
problems: Synthesizing short Python programs from natural language descriptions
Evaluating the ability of large language models to generate code in general-purpose programming languages
Assessing the impact of model size, prompt design, and fine-tuning on code synthesis performance
Understanding the limitations of language models in semantic grounding (predicting program outputs)
Improving code synthesis through human-in-the-loop feedback

results.metric: MBPP few-shot accuracy (largest model, no fine-tuning)
Error rate reduction with human feedback (MBPP, 4 dialog turns)
results.value: 50% reduction (from 30% to 65% accuracy)

novelty_claims: Introduction of two new benchmarks (MBPP and MathQA-Python) for evaluating program synthesis in Python from natural language.
Demonstration that large language models can synthesize correct Python code from natural language prompts, with performance scaling log-linearly with model size.
Showing that fine-tuning on a very small set of examples (374 problems) can yield significant improvements in synthesis accuracy.
First systematic study of interactive code synthesis with human-in-the-loop dialog, showing substantial error reduction from natural language feedback.
Comprehensive analysis of model performance factors (size, prompt, sampling, etc.) and evidence that solutions generalize beyond memorization.
Investigation into the semantic grounding of language models, revealing their limitations in predicting program outputs from inputs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): Freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture"],"problems":["Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks","Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large models","Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods","Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency"],"datasets":["RoBERTa","DeBERTa","GPT-2","GPT-3"],"metrics":["Number of trainable parameters","GPU memory requirement","Model quality (task-specific metrics, e.g., accuracy, perplexity, etc.)","Training throughput","Inference latency"],"results":[{"metric":"Reduction in trainable parameters (GPT-3 175B)","value":"10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Reduction in GPU memory requirement","value":"3x less GPU memory required compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Model quality (RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, GPT-3)","value":"On-par or better than full fine-tuning"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.","LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.","LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.","LoRA is orthogonal to and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.","Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA."]}

methods: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): Freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture
problems: Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks
Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large models
Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods
Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency
metrics: Model quality (task-specific metrics, e.g., accuracy, perplexity, etc.)

results.value: 10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning
3x less GPU memory required compared to full fine-tuning
On-par or better than full fine-tuning

novelty_claims: Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.
LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.
LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.
LoRA is orthogonal to and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.
Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA.

Generative pre-training (GPT) strategy for code generation
Support for code generation, code completion, code translation, and code explanation tasks

problems: Automatic program synthesis: generating executable code from human intent
Multilingual code generation and translation across multiple programming languages
Lack of benchmarks for evaluating functional correctness of code generation in multiple languages
Improving coding efficiency for programmers

results.value: 83.4% of users report increased efficiency using CodeGeeX extensions

novelty_claims: CodeGeeX is the first open-sourced 13B parameter multilingual code generation model trained on 23 programming languages and 850B tokens.
CodeGeeX supports not only code generation and completion but also code translation and explanation across multiple languages.
Development of HumanEval-X, the first benchmark for evaluating functional correctness of code generation and translation in Python, C++, Java, JavaScript, and Go.
CodeGeeX and its training process, model weights, API, and HumanEval-X benchmark are all open-sourced, unlike many prior large code models.
CodeGeeX-based IDE extensions support more diverse functions than Copilot and are widely adopted by users.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LESS (Low-rank gradiEnt Similarity Search): an optimizer-aware, efficient algorithm for instruction data selection that estimates data influences and selects data based on gradient similarity to few-shot target examples, adapted for Adam optimizer and variable-length instruction data, using LoRA and random projections to build a reusable low-dimensional gradient datastore."],"problems":["How to select the most relevant data from large instruction tuning datasets to develop specific capabilities (e.g., reasoning) in LLMs (targeted instruction tuning).","How to efficiently estimate data influence for instruction tuning with Adam optimizer and variable-length data.","How to enable transferable and interpretable data selection for targeted instruction tuning."],"datasets":["MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020)","TYDIQA (Clark et al., 2020)","BBH (Suzgun et al., 2023)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (on MMLU, TYDIQA, BBH and their subtasks)","Transferability of selected data (performance across model scales and families)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (LESS-selected 5% vs. full dataset)","value":"LESS-selected 5% of data often outperforms training on the full dataset across MMLU, TYDIQA, and BBH tasks (see Table 2 in the paper for specific numbers)."},{"metric":"Transferability","value":"Data selected using small models’ gradient features induce strong performance in large models and models from different families."}],"novelty_claims":["LESS is the first optimizer-aware data selection method for instruction tuning that adapts influence estimation to the Adam optimizer and variable-length instruction data.","LESS constructs a reusable, low-dimensional gradient datastore using LoRA and random projections, enabling efficient and transferable data selection.","LESS-selected data is highly transferable: small models can select data that is effective for larger models and different model families.","LESS goes beyond surface form cues, selecting data that exemplifies the necessary reasoning skills for the intended downstream application."]}

methods: LESS (Low-rank gradiEnt Similarity Search): an optimizer-aware, efficient algorithm for instruction data selection that estimates data influences and selects data based on gradient similarity to few-shot target examples, adapted for Adam optimizer and variable-length instruction data, using LoRA and random projections to build a reusable low-dimensional gradient datastore.
problems: How to select the most relevant data from large instruction tuning datasets to develop specific capabilities (e.g., reasoning) in LLMs (targeted instruction tuning).
How to efficiently estimate data influence for instruction tuning with Adam optimizer and variable-length data.
How to enable transferable and interpretable data selection for targeted instruction tuning.
metrics: Task accuracy (on MMLU, TYDIQA, BBH and their subtasks)
Transferability of selected data (performance across model scales and families)

results.value: LESS-selected 5% of data often outperforms training on the full dataset across MMLU, TYDIQA, and BBH tasks (see Table 2 in the paper for specific numbers).
Data selected using small models’ gradient features induce strong performance in large models and models from different families.

novelty_claims: LESS is the first optimizer-aware data selection method for instruction tuning that adapts influence estimation to the Adam optimizer and variable-length instruction data.
LESS constructs a reusable, low-dimensional gradient datastore using LoRA and random projections, enabling efficient and transferable data selection.
LESS-selected data is highly transferable: small models can select data that is effective for larger models and different model families.
LESS goes beyond surface form cues, selecting data that exemplifies the necessary reasoning skills for the intended downstream application.

improves not only accuracy but also usability (as judged by human raters) and responsible AI metrics.","Publicly releases Flan-T5 checkpoints with strong few-shot and CoT abilities."]}

problems: Improving generalization of language models to unseen tasks
Enhancing reasoning abilities of language models, especially with chain-of-thought prompting
Scaling instruction finetuning to more tasks and larger models
Improving multilingual and arithmetic reasoning abilities
Improving usability and performance of language models on open-ended generation tasks
datasets: A total of 473 datasets used for finetuning, including SQuAD and others
metrics: Accuracy on MGSM (arithmetic reasoning in under-represented languages)
Human rater evaluations on open-ended generation
Performance on responsible AI evaluation benchmarks

results.metric: Improvement on TyDiQA (one-shot, Flan-PaLM vs PaLM)
results.value: +14.9% absolute

results.metric: Improvement on MGSM (arithmetic reasoning, Flan-PaLM vs PaLM)
results.value: +8.1% absolute
Flan-T5 11B outperforms T5 11B by double-digit improvements and outperforms PaLM 62B on some tasks

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that instruction finetuning scales effectively with both the number of tasks and model size, leading to substantial performance improvements.
Shows that including a small amount of chain-of-thought data in the finetuning mixture enables strong reasoning abilities across all evaluations, overcoming prior degradation in CoT performance.
Trains and releases Flan-PaLM 540B and Flan-T5 models, achieving state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks and outperforming much larger models in some cases.
Provides evidence that instruction finetuning improves not only accuracy but also usability (as judged by human raters) and responsible AI metrics.
Publicly releases Flan-T5 checkpoints with strong few-shot and CoT abilities.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Language-model-based Evolutionary Optimizer (LEO): a population-based, parameter-free optimization method using LLMs to generate new candidate solutions, with an elitist framework separating explore and exploit pools of solutions"],"problems":["Black-box numerical optimization using LLMs","Multi-objective optimization","High-dimensional optimization problems","Application of LLMs to engineering optimization tasks (e.g., supersonic nozzle shape optimization, heat transfer, windfarm layout optimization)"],"datasets":["Benchmark numerical optimization problems (not named specifically)","Supersonic nozzle shape optimization dataset/problem","Heat transfer optimization dataset/problem","Windfarm layout optimization dataset/problem"],"metrics":["Optimization objective value (e.g., best found solution)","Comparison to state-of-the-art methods (relative performance)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the Language-model-based Evolutionary Optimizer (LEO), a novel population-based, parameter-free optimization strategy using LLMs with an elitist explore-exploit policy.","LEO distinguishes itself from other LLM-based optimization methods by its population-based approach and explicit separation of explore and exploit pools as guard rails.","Demonstration of LLMs' zero-shot optimization capability across diverse, including multi-objective and high-dimensional, problems.","Application of LEO to real-world engineering problems, showing comparable results to state-of-the-art optimization methods.","Provision of practical guidelines for reliable use of LLMs in optimization tasks."]}

methods: Language-model-based Evolutionary Optimizer (LEO): a population-based, parameter-free optimization method using LLMs to generate new candidate solutions, with an elitist framework separating explore and exploit pools of solutions
problems: Black-box numerical optimization using LLMs
Multi-objective optimization
High-dimensional optimization problems
Application of LLMs to engineering optimization tasks (e.g., supersonic nozzle shape optimization, heat transfer, windfarm layout optimization)
datasets: Benchmark numerical optimization problems (not named specifically)
metrics: Optimization objective value (e.g., best found solution)
novelty_claims: Introduction of the Language-model-based Evolutionary Optimizer (LEO), a novel population-based, parameter-free optimization strategy using LLMs with an elitist explore-exploit policy.
LEO distinguishes itself from other LLM-based optimization methods by its population-based approach and explicit separation of explore and exploit pools as guard rails.
Demonstration of LLMs' zero-shot optimization capability across diverse, including multi-objective and high-dimensional, problems.
Application of LEO to real-world engineering problems, showing comparable results to state-of-the-art optimization methods.
Provision of practical guidelines for reliable use of LLMs in optimization tasks.
